# Prefer Explicit Boundaries

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Principle

Engineering teams SHOULD make every significant component boundary visible,
documented, and subject to governance. Interfaces between components SHOULD
be defined by contracts — not by convention, tribal knowledge, or implicit
coupling.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C25 (Abstraction Leakage)** — Abstractions leak. When boundaries
  are implicit, leakage is invisible until it causes a production failure.
  Explicit boundaries make the leak surface inspectable so teams can detect
  and seal leaks before they propagate.
- **JOS-C24 (Cognitive Load)** — Every implicit boundary is a hidden
  dependency that a developer must discover and hold in working memory.
  Explicit boundaries convert discovery cost into lookup cost, reducing
  the cognitive load required to work safely in a codebase.

## Why This Matters

Implicit boundaries are the primary mechanism by which small, well-designed
systems degrade into tangled monoliths. When the line between "my
responsibility" and "your responsibility" is undocumented, both sides make
assumptions. Those assumptions diverge over time, producing integration
failures, duplicated logic, and unclear ownership. Explicit boundaries
create natural governance surfaces: they can be versioned, tested, and
monitored independently.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Boundary formalization has cost. Defining an interface contract for a
two-person team working on a single service may impose more ceremony than
it saves. The principle recognizes this via "expected" flexibility — teams
SHOULD formalize boundaries but may defer formalization for components that
are genuinely internal, small, and short-lived. The risk is that "temporary"
internal boundaries become permanent implicit contracts. Teams should
revisit deferred boundaries periodically.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, module boundaries were enforced by linker visibility rules. In
2070, boundaries may be enforced by AI-mediated contract verification.
The medium changes; the need for explicit demarcation does not. Systems
that hide their seams always surprise the people who maintain them.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles.

### Framework Connection

Framework §8 (five-contract boundary model — Dependency, Evidence, Interaction, Lifecycle, Mutation) provides the concrete taxonomy for what an explicit boundary looks like, converting the principle from a preference into a defined structural pattern.
